query: safety and efficacy of endoaortic balloon occlusion in atherosclerotic ascending aorta

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective management of aortoiliac occlusive disease (AIOD) requires a multidisciplinary approach to ensure optimal patient outcomes, enhance safety, and deliver patient-centered care. Physicians, including vascular surgeons and interventional radiologists, must utilize specialized skills in diagnostic imaging, decision-making regarding medical versus surgical intervention, and performing procedures like aortofemoral bypass or endovascular stenting. Advanced practitioners are key in preoperative evaluations, postoperative care, and patient education, ensuring adherence to lifestyle modifications and medical therapy. Nurses who monitor patients for signs of ischemia or complications facilitate patient education and contribute to ongoing assessments to ensure timely interventions, thus improving patient safety and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endovascular interventions, such as PTA, offer another treatment option with even lower in-hospital mortality of 0.6%. Although the long-term patency rates are slightly lower than those seen with open surgery, they remain high—96% at 1 year and 94% at 2 years—making endovascular treatment a less invasive alternative with a favorable safety profile. [44] Meta-analytic comparisons between open bypass and endovascular procedures show similar efficacy in limb salvage and survival, providing clinicians with multiple options tailored to individual patient needs (see Image. Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease Treatment Comparisons). [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Endovascular revascularization techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA): This technique uses an inflatable balloon catheter passed through a guidewire across the stenotic or occluded vessel. The balloon is inflated to compress the atherosclerotic plaque against the arterial wall, thereby widening the lumen. Primary patency rates after PTA are nearly 90% at 1 year, and primary assisted and secondary patency is 92.3%. [40] [41] [42] PTA can be performed with or without stent placement. Research has shown that covered stents provide better outcomes than bare-metal stents. [43] Endovascular treatments have also proven beneficial in challenging calcified lesions. [44] Thromboendarterectomy (TEA): TEA involves surgically removing atherosclerotic plaque directly from the arterial wall, typically performed with PTA. This procedure is more invasive than PTA but can be highly effective for patients with localized occlusive disease. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: After surgical intervention, the prognosis improves significantly. AFB surgery, a common procedure for severe AIOD, demonstrates excellent long-term outcomes with primary patency rates of 86.2% at 5 years and 77.6% at 10 years. The 10-year limb salvage and overall survival rates are 97.7% and 91.7%, respectively. [47] Although the 30-day mortality following AFB surgery is 2% to 3%, the long-term benefits make this an effective option for many patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Porcelain Aorta -- Treatment / Management -- Porcelain Aorta During Coronary Bypass Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: The other beneficial method is performing CABG during deep hypothermic circulatory arrest. In this practice, myocardial revascularization is performed using moderate hypothermia with a core temperature of 28-32°C for up to 40 minutes without harming the patient. In this case, sufficient time is given for the surgeon to perform safe proximal anastomoses without placing clamps on the aorta, minimizing the rate of cerebral complications. Salenger et al. [23] studied the outcome of this approach in 71 patients and found that one patient (1.4%) developed a mild stroke that resolved afterward, [26] analyzed six patients not suitable for clamping due to the severely calcified aorta. A deep hypothermic circulatory arrest for proximal anastomosis was made on all patients with complete recovery free of neurological problems.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians participating in this course can expect to gain comprehensive knowledge of the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of AIOD. They will learn to identify and assess risk factors, interpret diagnostic tests, and determine the most appropriate treatment options for individual patients, whether medical or surgical. The course will also emphasize interprofessional communication and care coordination, enhancing the ability of healthcare teams to provide patient-centered care, improve outcomes, and ensure patient safety throughout the treatment process.

retrieved_docs.title: Porcelain Aorta -- Treatment / Management -- Porcelain Aorta and Aortic Stenosis
retrieved_docs.content: Another new promising alternative is transcatheter aortic valve replacement ( TAVR) . It is the least invasive procedure to replace the aortic valve in high surgical risk patients with severe aortic stenosis. Patients having porcelain aorta will benefit from this approach by replacing the aortic valve without an aortic cross-clamping. In a study of around 60 patients with severe AS and PA, TAVR was successful in 98.4% of the patients with PA. The stroke and thirty-day mortality rates were 1.6% (1/61 patients) and 11.5% (7/61 patients), respectively, showing no differences from patients without PA. Pascual et al. stated that 449 patients underwent TAVR, of which 36 patients (8%) had a porcelain ascending aorta. The procedure was successful in 94.4%. The stroke and thirty-day mortality rates were 2.8% (1/36 patients) and 5.6% (2/36 patients), respectively, similar to patients without PA.

retrieved_docs.title: Porcelain Aorta -- Treatment / Management -- Porcelain Aorta During Coronary Bypass Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with porcelain aorta demonstrate an increased morbidity and mortality rate during CABG operation because of the elevated risk of embolic stroke from the atheromatous heavily calcified aorta. Such complication usually takes place during manipulation of the diseased aorta in three different maneuvers in CABG: Cannulation of the aorta Cross-clamping Partial clamping for the construction of the proximal anastomosis [4] Several amendments have been described to avoid cannulation and clamping of the porcelain aorta, including:

retrieved_docs.title: Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Choosing the Best Therapeutic Approach
retrieved_docs.content: The choice between open surgery and endovascular revascularization depends on several factors, including the extent of disease, patient comorbidities, and overall surgical risk. Open surgical approaches, like AFB, are often preferred for younger, healthier patients with long-segment or heavily calcified disease. In comparison, endovascular techniques are favored for older or high-risk patients due to their minimally invasive nature and lower in-hospital mortality rates. Both approaches have shown excellent long-term patency and limb salvage rates, and advances in endovascular techniques have further expanded treatment options for AIOD.

retrieved_docs.title: Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump -- Clinical Significance -- Role of IABP in High-Risk PCI
retrieved_docs.content: The application of IABP during the high-risk PCI in both stable and unstable patients was initially advocated based upon the low-quality evidence derived from the observational studies conducted by Mishra et al. and Briguori et al. [41] [42] However, subsequent randomized clinical trials did not demonstrate any difference in the in-hospital mortality, major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events between the patients undergoing high-risk PCI with or without the support of IABP.

retrieved_docs.title: Transluminal Extraction Coronary Atherectomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In the Pivotal Trial to Evaluate the Safety and Efficacy of the Orbital Atherectomy System in Treating De Novo, Severely Calcified Coronary Lesions (ORBIT II), a registry of 443 patients with severely calcified coronary lesions treated at 49 U.S. sites, the primary safety endpoint of freedom from 30-day MACE was achieved in 89.6% of patients. Stent delivery was successful in 97.7% of cases with less than 50% diameter stenosis (DS) achieved in 98.6% of subjects. Low rates of in-hospital Q-wave myocardial infarction (0.7%), cardiac death (0.2%), and TVR (0.7%) were reported. Angiographic complications included severe dissections in 15 patients (3.4%) and perforations in 8 patients (1.8%). [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Porcelain Aorta -- Treatment / Management -- Porcelain Aorta During Coronary Bypass Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: If there is a small non-calcified area confirmed by epiaortic ultrasound, a safe proximal anastomosis construction can be done using a proximal seal system [1] [25] [21] [16] to allow the surgeon to perform an anastomosis between the graft and the aorta without the need to do an aortic clamping. Hilker and his colleagues [32] published a study that reveals a stroke rate of 0.48% of 412 patients in which 542 had proximal anastomoses using a Heartstring device during CABG was performed. Dohmen G et al. [21] reported 25 successful anastomoses in 17 patients undergoing CABG. It was demonstrated that eight patients had porcelain aorta. Two of them developed neurological deficits after the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 922
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a manifestation of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, which is also known by the Arabic term تكلس أبهري (aortic calcification). This atherosclerotic disease is identified as a finding in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis). Peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis is recognized as an associated condition for the risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, indicating that systemic atherosclerotic burden can compromise cerebral blood flow. In addition, the risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion is linked to the presence of an atrial mucous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني), suggesting that embolic phenomena from cardiac tumors may further impair cerebral perfusion. Management of peripheral arterial disease frequently involves peripheral vascular bypass. The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) serves as a direct procedural site for this bypass, and the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) has a vascular relationship with the bypass procedure. Clinical examination of the femoral artery may reveal a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي), a finding that reflects turbulent flow secondary to arterial stenosis. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass appears in the surgical‑history record related to physical‑therapy treatment, as reflected by the answer mapping to the Chinese description of operative history; a similar mapping links the above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة) to that same surgical‑history entry. Together, abdominal aortic atherosclerosis, peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, femoral arterial findings, and the need for peripheral vascular bypass illustrate the interconnected nature of systemic atherosclerotic disease, its impact on cerebral perfusion, and the surgical interventions required for optimal patient care.

Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass appears in surgical histories and is associated with multidisciplinary care such as physical‑therapy‑related treatment. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the central role of the heart and aorta in rheumatic valve disease, systemic atherosclerotic disease, cerebral perfusion risk, and a range of cardiovascular diagnostic and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 829
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is identified as a pathological finding within the aorta abdominalis, indicating that atherosclerotic disease commonly involves the abdominal segment of the aorta. The aorta abdominalis itself is linked to peripheral vascular bypass, reflecting that surgical bypass procedures are often considered for restoring blood flow when significant aortic disease is present. In the lower extremity, the arteria femoralis (femoral artery) may present with لغط فخذي (thigh bruit), a clinical sign that suggests turbulent flow or stenosis in the femoral vessels. The femoral artery is also a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass, demonstrating that bypass surgery can be performed on the femoral artery to circumvent occlusive lesions. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass is associated with a surgical history that is relevant to physical‑therapy treatment, encompassing details such as the type of surgery, timing, and patient identifiers. Together, these relationships illustrate how atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta and femoral artery can lead to clinical findings (e.g., thigh bruit) and necessitate peripheral vascular bypass surgery, with the procedure’s history being integral to multidisciplinary patient management, including physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 152
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis, termed تكلس أبهري in Arabic, represents a manifestation of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis localized to the abdominal segment of the aorta and is associated with an increased risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, a risk also linked to atrial mucinous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني). The aorta serves as the primary anatomic site for peripheral and visceral atherosclerotic disease, functions as the parent vessel for arterial branches, and is the focus of several imaging and surgical interventions. Vascular imaging commonly employs arterial imaging techniques such as arteriografie, which encompasses contrast arteriograms of the femoral and lower‑extremity arteries and contrast aortograms; these are further refined by digital subtraction angiography (DSA) protocols ranging from ≤3 to ≥10 data‑acquisition runs, performed unilaterally or bilaterally, with specific protocols (e.g., bilateral 4‑to‑6‑run) directly mapped from contrast aortograms and the ≤3‑run unilateral DSA mapped to the aortic image (صورة الأبهر). The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is evaluated with contrast arteriograms, may present a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي) on clinical examination, and serves as a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass surgery. Surgical options for the aorta include resection, replacement, anastomosis, and direct peripheral vascular bypass, with the abdominal aorta specifically related to bypass procedures. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass appears in the patient’s surgical history and is associated with subsequent physical therapy. Collectively, these anatomical, imaging, and procedural relationships provide a comprehensive framework for assessing and managing vascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1162
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a pathological finding located in the aorta abdominalis. Because disease of the aorta abdominalis can compromise peripheral circulation, it is often associated with the need for a peripheral vascular bypass. The peripheral vascular bypass commonly uses the arteria femoralis as a direct procedural site to create an alternate pathway for blood flow. Consequently, a peripheral vascular bypass constitutes an element of the patient’s surgical history that is relevant to the planning and execution of physical‑therapy‑related treatment, as indicated by the associated record of surgical history linked to physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Digital subtraction angiography of aorta and lower limb, ≤3 data acquisition runs, bilateral; Digital subtraction angiography of aorta and lower limb, 4 to 6 data acquisition runs, bilateral; Digital subtraction angiography of aorta and lower limb, 7 to 9 data acquisition runs, bilateral; and Digital subtraction angiography of aorta and lower limb, 7 to 9 data acquisition runs, unilateral are all primary sources for generating a contrast aortogram. These various DSA protocols, differing by the number of acquisition runs and by whether imaging is performed bilaterally or unilaterally, provide the essential contrast‑enhanced vascular images of the aorta and lower extremities that are used for diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and follow‑up of vascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a disease that manifests in the aorta abdominalis, indicating that the abdominal segment of the aorta is the typical site where atherosclerotic plaques develop. Because the aorta abdominalis is a major conduit for blood flow to the lower extremities, it is also a focal point in the planning and execution of peripheral vascular bypass procedures, which are performed to restore adequate circulation when peripheral arterial disease compromises blood supply. Recognizing the presence of abdominal aortic atherosclerosis and its impact on the aorta abdominalis is therefore essential for clinicians when evaluating patients for peripheral vascular bypass surgery, as it influences both diagnostic assessment and surgical strategy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is a specialized therapeutic procedure for heart diseases (الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب). However, CABG is contraindicated in the presence of the disease ketorolak, indicating that patients diagnosed with ketorolak should not undergo this surgical intervention. This knowledge helps clinicians select appropriate candidates for CABG and avoid performing the procedure when ketorolak is present, thereby improving patient safety and treatment outcomes.

This connection is essential for planning and executing revascularization strategies in patients with peripheral arterial disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 971
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is considered a manifestation of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis and is found in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis). Peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, which is also referred to by the Arabic term تكلس أبهري (aortic calcification), is identified as an associated condition that contributes to the risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion. Moreover, the risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion is linked as an associated condition of an atrial mucinous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني). Together, these relationships highlight how atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta and broader peripheral/visceral atherosclerosis can impact cerebral perfusion and may be relevant in the clinical context of cardiac tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 827
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is identified as a pathological finding located in the abdominal aorta. The abdominal aorta is directly involved in peripheral vascular bypass procedures, which are surgical interventions that create an alternative pathway for blood flow (إراقة الدم) through the vascular system. Vessels (vény) are generally associated with peripheral vascular bypass, serving as indirect sites that facilitate the rerouting of blood. The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass and may present clinically with a femoral bruit (لغط فخذي), indicating turbulent flow at that location. The history of undergoing a peripheral vascular bypass is recorded as part of the patient’s surgical background that is relevant to subsequent physical therapy management. Together, these relationships highlight how atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta, vascular bypass surgery, and associated findings in the femoral artery are interconnected considerations in comprehensive vascular care and rehabilitation planning.
